speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rosner, you were telling me when the Russians jumped off the train. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train moved. And we-- after a couple days traveling on that train, we came luckily to Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Cracow, we start to look for my family. There was a committee of survivors of Holocaust, start-- inquire. But unfortunately, I didn't found anybody of my family. I learned that my sister [? Mary ?] was taken on the boat in middle of the sea with some other woman-- sunk. I learned that my brother Sam was in Mauthausen and died one day before the end of the war from hunger. And I didn't know what this-- with my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my wife didn't know what happened to her sister. But I started to play in the nightclub in Cracow-- a nightclub.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did-- did you visit your old home?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my-- the place of birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you used-- did you visit the old house where you used to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I didn't. I visit the outside now when I've been, five years ago. It's-- I live on the ground floor, the window coming on the street. I, I, I seen the place but not inside. It was just small, small kitchen and, and, and big room.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to play in nightclub Casanova. And we lived very close by. My sister-in-law in the same house lived there. And my wife was afraid to stay at home alone. So she was coming with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one afternoon while they're playing, my wife was sitting in corner lodge. One tall Russian officer, drunk, come to my wife. He says in Russian, come dance. She says, don't want.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said very rude word, took out the gun. I liberate-- you don't want to dance with me? And some people jump on him and stop him. He want to kill her. [LAUGHS] Some dumb drunk, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, he-- we've been there about a year in Cracow. And we learned that my brother is in Munich, in Germany, Munchen. Also [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my wife heard the news about her sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met in, in Munich. We went to Munich. And I play with my brother on the stage in the theater [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few weeks, we met Oskar Schindler in Munich. And he lived with us in one apartment for about two months, quite-- about eight people, big apartment. And he was with his Jew girlfriend-- Jewish girlfriends. And he was still drinking cognac and smoking. He spoke few word in Yiddish and singing the Viennese-- I play accordion, singing. And my brother got a permit to go to America. Because my other brother was in America from before the war, he sent him permit, and also my youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit more about Germany? What was it like being in Germany after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, after the war, I play in Deutsches Museum. This is Jewish-- German museum-- called. a very big place-- there was meeting place for all the displaced person, all the concentration camp, and all nationalities coming there, making some black market, you know, selling dollars, you know-- black market. I was never good in, in, in making business. But my brother was-- Henry. And that there was one Polish waiter. While I was playing, he comes, Rosner, Rosner, show me-- show me that. I had a gold-- beautiful gold watch. Show me that watch. I got somebody. I took it off, give it to him. I never seen him again, never seen the watch again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So in that Deutsches Museum, we've been playing for a few months. And then my sister-in-law got a permit to France, to Paris. Although I would like to go to America to be with my brothers, but my wife wants to go to be with her sister. And I want to be with my wife. So I went to Paris. Can you tell me, was that the last time you saw Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. In Paris, he visit us one day-- in Paris and brought a dozen red roses for Helene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you think he was changed in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was drinking still. He was charming man. See, he was using a lot of aftershave. When he passed by, five minutes after, you can smell he passed [LAUGHS] by. He was very handsome, tall, good-looking man. I mean, after, no-- you see on the photos, he, he went down from drinking. His liver gave up. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Paris. I got straight away work. You see, in Paris, I went to musicians union [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to get permit. So you can be hairdresser. You can be writer. You can be anything, but not music-- we've got too many musicians. But I got a job straight away. I play in, in a-- with well-known, Jewish Russian orchestra, [? Yakovlev-- ?] [? Jakovlev-- ?] [? Yakovlev ?] in very big restaurant. Then I play in Petit [INAUDIBLE], French restaurant, cabaret, owned by a friend of mine. Then they play on the stage with composer of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my god, the names-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yves Montand was on stage and [INAUDIBLE], famous dancer, on the same stage. And I play with this gypsy band on the stage. And Django Reinhardt, Stephane Grappelli-- very famous guitar player and violinist, Django, playing on the same stage. And there-- I stay in Paris for three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the atmosphere in the-- in the restaurants when, when you played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good atmosphere. I play not-- pardon me-- not only in Jewish place. I play in, in Cafe de la Republique. I play big cafe. Then I play in Montmartre restaurant. One day, one French man came to the band and asked us to play that tune, 'Gloomy Sunday.' He gave us 5,000 francs. That wasn't so much. But it sounds very much, quite a lot. He ordered a bottle of soda water. We played the tune. He left. And I don't know what's happened to him-- that, that same tune, 'Gloomy Sunday.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your feelings during this time after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was glad to be alive. I-- when I came to France, I didn't spoke one word. Now I can speak quite a bit of French. And I play with some new musicians, some very, very good musicians. There is accordionist-- thousands, especially in France, accordionists. But I wasn't one day without work. I remember once older chap came to me, that he's very nervous. He was to doctor. He asked-- he suggested he buy a little dog or learn some instrument. So I said, buy a dog. [LAUGHTER] And my daughter Anna was born in Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very hard, you see. I play till early morning. We had one room in kitchen. In that room slept my sister-in-law with her husband. And she give birth also to a son, [? Louis, ?] five weeks different than my daughter Anna. And I slept in kitchen on the bed with my wife. And Anna slept in, in, in other room with them. And my sister-in-law didn't have enough milk. My wife was breastfeeding her son. He is 6 foot. Here, he is a solicitor. [LAUGHTER] And I slept in the kitchen. And children crying and cooking-- I bought myself earplugs to put into ear, so I could sleep in daytime. And I survived. It's-- after concentration camp is nothing too hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to practice, practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: --practice at night-- all night is enough practice. Who got time for this? See, that's, that's the greatest practice that then you can practice at home, you see. As I said before, in the nightclub, I play from 5:00 to 8:00 and from 9:00 till morning. So that's many hours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stay in Paris three and a half years. And my sister-in-law got a permit to Australia. So we applied for a permit. Luckily, we got the permission. We came to this beautiful country. How, how did you apply for the permit?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Australian Consulate. And I didn't have-- I came as a Nansen passport, this-- no, no nationality. See, I reduced-- I refused my nationality in Germany, my Polish nationality, when I want-- I want to come to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't have nationality. So they give me temporary passport. And I came to Australia in December, '49. Did it cost you anything to come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing, no bribing, no, no, bribing, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wait a long time--

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: To get the permit--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to get the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Short time. Wasn't long, no. And my sister-in-law wait for us at the airport-- at the-- we came by boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do Do you remember the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's Greek boat. We went from France-- from Paris, we went to Genova-- Geneva. And from there, by boat, we traveled about 28, 29 days on the boat. We came luckily to Australia. I was-- I am very fortunate that I follow my sister-in-law, that I came to this beautiful country. I'm very happy here. And my second daughter Frances is born here. And my six grandchildren are born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Fremantle-- we stopped Fremantle. But I came to Melbourne with the boat. And from that time, I didn't-- I-- my brother-in-law bought a house in Glen Huntly Road in Caulfield, quite a big house. We moved together. At that time, we weren't allowed to bring any money. And I had couple thousand of dollars when I came to Australia. So I got idea. I had a half of bread-- rye bread. I cut off a slice and dig out the middle. I put the money in, put the slice back with toothpicks. And I smuggled that money here. It was very handy. We gave deposit on this house. And we paid it off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was quite a big house, big garden, back garden, front garden. We weren't used to such a big gardens. I didn't have a car, of course, then. And never forget this-- the first year in Australia, I play some Australian party. I play double bass and accordion. I didn't have a car. One Australian offer himself to give me a lift. It took me half an hour to put the big bass into car, you know, across. It was-- it was a long instrument-- put the accordion. He was a bit drunk. He's driving about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] half a kilometer. He said, get out. I haven't got petrol. He throw me out of middle of nowhere. I couldn't hardly speak English. Now, with the accordion and bass, look for telephone book-- booth. If I got telephone booth, I have to have small change for telephone. If I have small change for telephone, I have to speak English. If I speak English, have to say to contacts you where, where I am at night. [LAUGHS] I don't remember how I came home. And was of course, very hard from beginning. I bought a mattress. We slept on the floor. We sat on the fruit boxes-- wooden fruit boxes. Slowly, I was working hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Private parties mostly-- private parties. And I met very good friend, Australian Denis Farrington, very righteous person, very religious Catholic. He like drink. But when come Easter time, Lent, he wouldn't touch. That's a strong willpower. But he [LAUGHS] make it up after. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got a proposition from one Jewish organization to play 12-piece band in St. Kilda Town Hall. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was a member of the union. It was such a lot. You have to be five years in Australia to become member before you have to make exam. So I didn't have membership.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one from union came to me. Mr. Rosner, I know that you are a very good musician. But unfortunately, you are not allowed to play with union musicians. I says, all right. I got idea. I booked this Denis Farrington, six musicians, and six newcomer on the same stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: They play four numbers. And I play with my band four numbers. They couldn't touch me. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was plenty of work, plenty jobs. Finally, after five years, I applied for membership. I went to union. One lady [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] asked me to play something, said, I wish I could play like you. And I got the membership. That was 46 years ago-- or but, well, 41 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never got a job from union. But once helped me that-- I got a membership little thing here. And once I lost the whole bunch of keys. I lost-- and one woman found the keys. And there is membership number. She rang to union. They, they advised me-- oh, that one, member of union. And they rang me. [LAUGHS] I got the keys. That was good about this. My second daughter Frances was born here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was very hard from beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: --help you? Did any organization help you? Did you receive any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never asked anybody-- never asked anybody. And I play with Denis Farrington a lot of-- I play for prime ministers and for Holt, who disappeared, got-- he was drowned, I think. It was big, big affair here. I got a letter from him. And I play quite a lot of minsters, with-- and very important people I play a lot of-- I play wedding for Rabbi Gutnick, one son-- three sons. I think that one is that, that multi-millionaire, that Gutnick, that diamond merchant. I think there was also one of the sons. It was a very big family. And slowly, slowly, I went into partnership with one person and opened nightclub Moulin Rouge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Elwood, not far. And up there was a nightclub open only once a week on Saturday from beginning. But the liquor law was very harsh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't have license to, to sell liquor, didn't have license even for people to bring their own liquor. You have to apply. And that license only applies for Saturday till 11 o'clock-- till 12 o'clock because after 12:00 is Sunday. You're not allowed. Once a New Year's Eve came on Saturday. And to celebrate, it's 12 o'clock at night-- not allowed to. They warned me they will come to check-- not allowed to.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came and caught-- some people had some glass from-- that smell, smell of alcohol. I said, what's it supposed to smell of? They've been drinking before, was before 12:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I got summons to court. The maximum penalty was $50. I took a solicitor. The court case and blah, blah, blah, blah, they let me free. And I got the bill from the solicitor, 80, 80 pounds. With a maximum of 50 pounds, got 80 pounds. [LAUGHS] And so, you know, then I make a catering in there under supervision of beth din kosher catering-- had quite a lot of weddings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then in '73, I think, I bought-- with the same partner, I bought a place-- we built a place called [? Diane ?] Receptions. It was on Clarence Street, see, not far from here, quite a big place. And they had quite a lot of wedding-- Jewish weddings, some non-Jewish weddings. But was very hard still with this permit-- drinking permit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it wasn't good-- doing good, that place. And finally, I bought out my partner. And I sold this business for lousy money. I could hardly buy a flat for this money what I got for this building-- standing still there. Anyhow, about the Schindler. A friend of mine, Poldek Pfefferberg, Paul Page, we've been together in concentration camp. He's from Cracow also. I visit him in Los Angeles. I've been eight times in America to visit my brothers. I visited him in Los Angeles. And he had on the counter a lot of photos and books and papers. He wanted to make a film but wasn't-- nobody was interested. And one day by accident, Thomas Keneally, the writer of the or came by accident in Los Angeles to that shop, had broken travel case. He came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: While he was waiting, he said, where you come from? He said, from Australia. What are you doing? I'm a writer. Are you a good writer? He said, I think so. They pay me for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have I got a story for you! He took from under the counter the papers. He make him interested in the story. They make agreement. They started to go around the world, have interviews with the survivors. Of course, Thomas Keneally was a few times in this place here, had the interview with me few time. Helene didn't want to talk. She was-- she had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was very, very nervous. She couldn't talk about her previous years in, in concentration camp. She went over to the kitchen. Now she changed completely. She had-- interviewed today. And the book came out. He got the book-- his price.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you-- did you feel when you read the book? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very, very good. Very, very right. Very good, very cleverly written book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel it was true?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, yes, very good. And there is a-- in the book, first book, originally printed in America, is a bigger size. And with the-- I got it up there on the table. And with the sort of a picture, chapter 7 is written a lot about us, my brother and I-- and a picture of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where the book was written, one night I got a telephone from London from Thames Television. Mr. Rosner, we'd like to make documentary about your story, about the Schindler. Would you like to come to New York, see your brother? I said, I would love to.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was in '83. And I told them the story about my honeymoon, which I didn't have. It was at this-- exactly that time in January. So I wouldn't go without my wife because I promised we would celebrate especially that night. I'm sorry. We can't afford. All right, put the phone back. And five minutes, he rings back from London. Mr. Rosner, we pay everything. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to London-- I beg your pardon. I came to New York. My brother waited at the airport. He said, Pol, did I tell you funny thing? There's a strike of cameramen in London. They're not allowed to come to America. We have to flee to, to London. So we flew to London. The black limousine was waiting for us at the airport. And we were very nicely welcomed. We had the interview on television for Thames Television. It was very successful also. And I play with my brother Henry. And then the film got also first prize. Did you feel the film was as good as the book? Or how did you feel when you saw yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the film?

speaker: interviewee
text: --documentary. Oh, it was interviews mostly. That wasn't film-- interviews. Also interview with girlfriend of Amon Goeth. She had emphysema. She hardly could speak. And she spoke about Amon Goeth in superlative, that he wasn't anti-Semite this, blah. And next day, she died from emphysema. And also interview with Mrs. Schindler. She spoke out about-- she wasn't very, very favorable talking about her husband because he was unfaithful to her for all married life. Even on the night of the wedding, he was arrested. Some other one that-- he live with her-- tapped him for something. He was arrested on the night of the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these things that happened to you in the war, had you told your children about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children-- --not much. --know already?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we had the first interview with Olle, the one who died recently from channel 2. I got the tape here, Andrew Olle. And when my daughter seen interview-- Helene saying-- talking about this Auschwitz, how her drank water from gutters.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, Mom, for God's sake, why didn't you tell us this? From that time, she unwind. She start to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: We told them all. They are very interested. My granddaughters are very interested. Even they're talking about this in schools. And I'm invited a lot of times to school to talk about my experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: But very unpleasant thing happened after the book [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One writer from girly magazine, Australian magazine, ring me. Mr. Rosner, I'd like to have interview with you. It's through the phone? Yes. So did you read the book? No. So you read the book first. Then we'll have interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she rings me after a week. Mr. Rosner, I read the book. Because you took out of context that story about the suicide.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, Mr. Rosner, is it true that one German commits suicide listening to music? I said, yes. Thank you. Bang. That was the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Week after came this magazine. The first page-- 'Holocaust survivors used music to murder.' And inside, picture of my brother and I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my wife and Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hold that straight up--

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is written, 'Jewish brother Henry and Leo Rosner commit the perfect crime against an SS officer in World War II concentration camp-- murdered by music. They played melancholy Hungarian tune so often and with such a feeling that they drove the hated SS man to shoot himself.' I was very much-- very angry. I wrote a very strong letter I got apologies. But the damage is done. And I wrote to my brother, sent him this paper. He said, Leo, don't be silly! We should be proud that we play with such a feeling! [LAUGHTER]
